response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), effective May 2018, applies to any organization processing the personal data of EU residents and mandates rights such as access, erasure, data portability, and explicit consent, while requiring breach reporting within 72 hours and appointing Data Protection Officers for large‑scale processing. Violations can incur fines of up to 4 % of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher), exemplified by Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million penalty, and Google’s cumulative fines exceeding €200 million. GDPR has become a global model, inspiring laws like California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, though critics note compliance burdens on small firms, inconsistent enforcement, and user fatigue from cookie banners. Despite these challenges, the regulation has cemented data privacy as a fundamental right and set the worldwide standard for data protection.
